欽明天皇稜に隣接する吉備姫皇女古墳内にある奇石。ユニークな人面石像であるが、猿ではなく渡来人を象ったものであるといわれている。
'''猿石'''（さるいし）は、欽明天皇稜に隣接する吉備姫皇女古墳内にある奇石。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。

{{即時削除|定義未満}}
'''猿石'''（さるいし）は、欽明天皇稜に隣接する吉備姫皇女古墳内にある奇石。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。
'''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[欽明天皇稜]]に隣接する吉備姫皇女古墳内にある奇石。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。

'''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[欽明天皇陵]]に隣接する吉備姫皇女古墳内にある奇石。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。もとは欽明天皇陵に置かれていたらしいが，明治初年ごろに移された。[[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれている。
[[Category:奈良県の建築物・観光名所|さるいし]] [[Category:明日香村|さるいし]] {{stub}}

'''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[梅山古墳]]（現:欽明天皇陵指定）に隣接する吉備姫皇女古墳内にある奇石で、全部で4体ある。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。
==詳細== 元禄2年（1702年）に梅山古墳付近の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが，明治初年ごろに現在の場所に移されたらしい。柵でしきられているため間近で見ることはできないが、[[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれている。
4体の像にはその外見から「女」「山王権現」「僧」「男」とそれぞれに愛称がつけられおり、山王権現と女は像の背面にも顔が彫られている二面石である。
==高取の猿石== [[高取城]]への登山道の途中にも猿石と呼ばれる石像が1体置かれている。もともと上記の4体と同じ場所から掘り出されたが、高取城の石垣をつくる石材を運ぶ際に、現在の場所に移されたのではないかと考えられている。
[[Category:奈良県の建築物・観光名所|さるいし]] [[Category:明日香村|さるいし]] {{stub}}

'''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[梅山古墳]]（現:欽明天皇陵指定）に隣接する吉備姫皇女古墳内にある奇石で、全部で4体ある。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。
==詳細== *元禄2年（1702年）に梅山古墳付近の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、明治初年ごろに現在の場所に移されたらしい。柵でしきられているため間近から見ることはできないが、[[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれている。
*4体の像にはその外見から「女」「山王権現」「僧（法師）」「男」とそれぞれに愛称がつけられおり、山王権現・女・男には像の背面にも顔が彫られている二面石である。僧の背面には肋骨らしきものが彫られている。
==高取の猿石== [[高取城]]への登山道の途中にも猿石と呼ばれる石像が1体置かれている。もともと上記の4体と同じ場所から掘り出されたが、高取城の石垣をつくる石材を運ぶ際に、現在の場所に移されたのではないかと考えられている。
[[Category:奈良県の建築物・観光名所|さるいし]] [[Category:明日香村|さるいし]] {{stub}}

revision.text:
'''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[梅山古墳]]（現:欽明天皇陵指定）の西に隣接する吉備姫皇女王墓内にある奇石で、全部で4体ある。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。 [[画像:Saruisi s+m.jpg|thumb|200px|山王権現（手前）と女（奥）]] [[画像:Saruisi m+h.jpg|thumb|200px|僧（手前）と男（奥）]]
==詳細== 1702年（元禄2年）に梅山古墳の付近にあった平田村池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、明治初年ごろに現在の場所に移されたらしい。4体の像にはその外見から左から順に『女』『山王権現』『僧（法師）』『男』とそれぞれに愛称がつけられている。僧以外は像の背面にも顔が彫られている二面石で、僧の背面には肋骨らしきものが彫られている。
柵でしきられているため前からしか見ることはできないが、[[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。
==高取の猿石== [[高取城]]への登山道の途中にも猿石と呼ばれる石像が1体置かれている。もともと上記の4体と同じ場所から掘り出され、その後現在の場所に移されたのではないかといわれているが、確かなことは分かっていない。
==関連項目== *[[飛鳥の石造物]]
==外部リンク== *[http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石]
{{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の建築物・観光名所]] [[Category:明日香村]] {{japanese-history-stub}}

'''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[梅山古墳]]（現:欽明天皇陵指定）の西に隣接する吉備姫皇女王墓内にある奇石で、全部で4体ある。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。 [[画像:Saruisi s+m.jpg|thumb|200px|山王権現（手前）と女（奥）]] [[画像:Saruisi m+h.jpg|thumb|200px|僧（手前）と男（奥）]]
==詳細== 1702年（元禄2年）に梅山古墳の付近にあった平田村池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、明治初年ごろに現在の場所に移されたらしい。4体の像にはその外見から左から順に『女』『山王権現』『僧（法師）』『男』とそれぞれに愛称がつけられている。僧以外は像の背面にも顔が彫られている二面石で、僧の背面には肋骨らしきものが彫られている。
柵でしきられているため前からしか見ることはできないが、[[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。
==高取の猿石== [[高取城]]への登山道の途中にも猿石と呼ばれる石像が1体置かれている。もともと上記の4体と同じ場所から掘り出され、その後現在の場所に移されたのではないかといわれているが、確かなことは分かっていない。
== 交通 == [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[飛鳥駅]]徒歩10分
==関連項目== *[[飛鳥の石造物]] *[[吉備姫]]
==外部リンク== *[http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石]
{{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:明日香村]] {{japanese-history-stub}}

{{Double image aside|right|Saruisi s+m.jpg|180|Saruisi m+h.jpg|180|山王権現（手前）と女（奥）|僧（手前）と男（奥）}} '''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]の[[梅山古墳]]（現:欽明天皇陵指定）西に隣接する[[吉備姫王|吉備姫皇女王]]墓内にある奇石で、全部で4体ある。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。
==詳細== 1702年（元禄2年）に梅山古墳の付近にあった平田村池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、明治初年ごろに現在の場所に移されたらしい。4体の像にはその外見から左から順に『[[女]]』『[[山王権現]]』『[[僧]]（[[法師]]）』『[[男]]』とそれぞれに愛称がつけられている。僧以外は像の背面にも顔が彫られている二面石で、僧の背面には肋骨らしきものが彫られている。
柵でしきられているため前からしか見ることはできないが、[[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。
写真からも分かるように、一部の像には[[性器]]を露わにしたと解釈できるものがある。
==高取の猿石== [[高取城]]への登山道の途中にも猿石と呼ばれる石像が1体置かれている。もともと上記の4体と同じ場所から掘り出され、その後現在の場所に移されたのではないかといわれているが、確かなことは分かっていない。
== 交通 == [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[飛鳥駅]]徒歩10分
==関連項目== *[[飛鳥の石造物]]
==外部リンク== *[http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石]
{{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:石像]]

revision.comment: 出土と現在地移動出典貼付し調整記述、猪熊兼勝 百済石人像類似説

{{出典の明記|date=2018-06-12}} {{Four images|right|Saruishi-1.jpg|120|Saruishi-2.jpg|120|{{center|上段：女（左）・山王権現（右）
下段：僧（左）・男（右）}}||Saruishi-3.jpg|Saruishi-4.jpg}} '''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]の[[吉備姫王]]墓内にある奇石。全4体。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。 == 概要 == 1702年（元禄15年）10月5日に梅山古墳の付近にあった坂合村大字平田字池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、古墳修築時にいったん他に移動させて、その後に現在の場所に移された今西伊之吉他6名『奈良県高市郡志料』奈良県高市郡役所（編集・刊行）1915年、pp.299-300。 [[猪熊兼勝]]は、[[百済]][[益山市|益山]]の[[弥勒寺]]跡（[[:en:Mireuksa|英語版]]）の西石塔の四隅に置かれた石人像と類似し百済工人の技術が使用され、7世紀後半の作造と推定する猪熊兼勝「飛鳥猿石考」『奈良国立文化財研究所創立30周年記念論文集文化財論叢』1983年。 4体の像にはその外見から左から順に「[[女]]」・「[[山王権現]]」・「[[僧]]（[[法師]]）」・「[[男]]」とそれぞれに愛称がつけられている。僧以外は像の背面にも顔が彫られている二面石で、僧の背面には肋骨らしきものが彫られている。 柵でしきられているため前からしか見ることはできないが、[[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。 写真からも分かるように、一部の像には[[性器]]を露わにしたと解釈できるものがある。 == 高取の猿石 == [[高取城]]への登山道の途中にも猿石と呼ばれる石像が1体置かれている。もともと上記の4体と同じ場所から掘り出され、その後現在の場所に移されたのではないかといわれているが、確かなことは分かっていない。 == 交通 == [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[飛鳥駅]]徒歩10分 == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥の石造物]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石] {{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:石像]]

下段：僧（左）・男（右）}}||Saruishi-3.jpg|Saruishi-4.jpg}} '''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]の[[吉備姫王]]墓内にある奇石。全4体。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。 == 概要 == 1702年（元禄15年）10月5日に梅山古墳の付近にあった坂合村大字平田字池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、古墳修築時にいったん他に移動させて、その後に現在の場所に移された今西伊之吉他6名『奈良県高市郡志料』奈良県高市郡役所（編集・刊行）1915年、pp.299-300。 [[猪熊兼勝]]は、[[百済]][[益山市|益山]]の[[弥勒寺]]跡（[[:en:Mireuksa|英語版]]）の西石塔の四隅に置かれた石人像と類似し百済工人の技術が使用され、7世紀後半の作造と推定する猪熊兼勝「飛鳥猿石考」『奈良国立文化財研究所創立30周年記念論文集文化財論叢』1983年。 4体の像にはその外見から左から順に「[[女]]」・「[[山王権現]]」・「[[僧]]（[[法師]]）」・「[[男]]」とそれぞれに愛称がつけられている。僧以外は像の背面にも顔が彫られている二面石で、僧の背面には肋骨らしきものが彫られている。 柵でしきられているため前からしか見ることはできないが、[[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。 写真からも分かるように、一部の像には[[性器]]を露わにしたと解釈できるものがある。 == 高取の猿石 == [[高取城]]への登山道の途中にも猿石と呼ばれる石像が1体置かれている。もともと上記の4体と同じ場所から掘り出され、その後現在の場所に移されたのではないかといわれているが、確かなことは分かっていない。 == 交通 == [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[飛鳥駅]]徒歩10分 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥の石造物]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石] {{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:石像]]

{{出典の明記|date=2018-06-12}} {{Four images|right|Saruishi-1.jpg|120|Saruishi-2.jpg|120|{{center|上段：女（左）・山王権現（右）
下段：僧（左）・男（右）}}||Saruishi-3.jpg|Saruishi-4.jpg}} '''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]の[[吉備姫王]]墓内にある奇石。全4体。ユニークな人面石像であるが、[[サル|猿]]ではなく[[渡来人]]を象ったものであるといわれている。 == 概要 == [[1702年]]（元禄15年）10月5日に梅山古墳の付近にあった坂合村大字平田字池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、古墳修築時にいったん他に移動させて、その後に現在の場所に移された今西伊之吉他6名『奈良県高市郡志料』奈良県高市郡役所（編集・刊行）1915年、pp.299-300。 [[猪熊兼勝]]は、[[百済]][[益山市|益山]]の[[弥勒寺]]跡（[[:en:Mireuksa|英語版]]）の西石塔の四隅に置かれた石人像と類似し百済工人の技術が使用され、7世紀後半の作造と推定する猪熊兼勝「飛鳥猿石考」『奈良国立文化財研究所創立30周年記念論文集文化財論叢』1983年。 4体の像にはその外見から左から順に「[[女]]」・「[[山王権現]]」・「[[僧]]（[[法師]]）」・「[[男]]」とそれぞれに愛称がつけられている。僧以外は像の背面にも顔が彫られている二面石で、僧の背面には肋骨らしきものが彫られている。 柵でしきられているため前からしか見ることはできないが、[[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。 写真からも分かるように、一部の像には[[性器]]を露わにしたと解釈できるものがある。 == 高取の猿石 == [[高取城]]への登山道の途中にも猿石と呼ばれる石像が1体置かれている。もともと上記の4体と同じ場所から掘り出され、その後現在の場所に移されたのではないかといわれているが、確かなことは分かっていない。 == 交通 == [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[飛鳥駅]]徒歩10分 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥の石造物]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石] {{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:石像]]

{{Four images|right|Saruishi-1.jpg|120|Saruishi-2.jpg|120|{{center|上段：女（左）・山王権現（右）
下段：僧（左）・男（右）}}||Saruishi-3.jpg|Saruishi-4.jpg}} '''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]の[[吉備姫王]]墓伝承地内にある奇石。全4体。 == 概要 == [[梅山古墳]]（欽明天皇陵古墳）の南西には吉備姫墓伝承地とされる円墳（檜隈墓）があり、その墓域内に花崗岩の4体の石像が西向きに南北に並んでいる{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=飛鳥地域の古墳（西部・東部）|accessdate=2024年3月12日|publisher=奈良県教育委員会事務局 文化財保存課}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=遠藤祐二 |author2=加藤碵一 |title=飛鳥の石造物 |journal=地質ニュース |issue=592 |publisher= |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}。4体の像は北から順に通称で「[[女性]]」・「[[山王権現]]」・「[[法師]]」・「[[男性]]」と名付けられている。このうち「法師」については[[力士]]とする説もある。また「法師」像以外は裏面にも顔が彫られているとされるが、墓域のため柵があり現地では確認できない。 [[1702年]]（元禄15年）10月5日に梅山古墳の付近にあった坂合村大字平田字池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、古墳修築時にいったん他に移動させて、その後に現在の場所に移された今西伊之吉他6名『奈良県高市郡志料』奈良県高市郡役所（編集・刊行）1915年、pp.299-300。 [[猪熊兼勝]]は、[[百済]][[益山市|益山]]の[[弥勒寺]]跡（[[:en:Mireuksa|英語版]]）の西石塔の四隅に置かれた石人像と類似し百済工人の技術が使用され、7世紀後半の作造と推定する猪熊兼勝「飛鳥猿石考」『奈良国立文化財研究所創立30周年記念論文集文化財論叢』1983年。 [[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。 == 高取の猿石 == [[高取城]]への登山道の途中にも猿石と呼ばれる石像が1体置かれている。もともと上記の4体と同じ場所から掘り出され、その後現在の場所に移されたのではないかといわれているが、確かなことは分かっていない。 == 交通 == * [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[飛鳥駅]]徒歩10分 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥の石造物]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石] {{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:石像]]

{{Four images|right|Saruishi-1.jpg|120|Saruishi-2.jpg|120|{{center|上段：女（左）・山王権現（右）
下段：僧（左）・男（右）}}||Saruishi-3.jpg|Saruishi-4.jpg}} '''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]の[[吉備姫王]]墓伝承地内にある奇石。全4体。 == 概要 == [[梅山古墳]]（欽明天皇陵古墳）の南西には吉備姫墓伝承地とされる円墳（檜隈墓）があり、その墓域内に花崗岩の4体の石像が西向きに南北に並んでいる{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=飛鳥地域の古墳（西部・東部）|accessdate=2024年3月12日|publisher=奈良県教育委員会事務局 文化財保存課}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=遠藤祐二 |author2=加藤碵一 |title=飛鳥の石造物 |journal=地質ニュース |issue=592 |publisher= |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}。4体の像は北から順に通称で「[[女性]]」・「[[山王権現]]」・「[[法師]]」・「[[男性]]」と名付けられている。このうち「法師」については[[力士]]とする説もある。また「法師」像以外は裏面にも顔が彫られているとされるが、墓域のため柵があり現地では確認できない。 [[1702年]]（元禄15年）10月5日に梅山古墳の付近にあった坂合村大字平田字池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、古墳修築時にいったん他に移動させて、その後に現在の場所に移された今西伊之吉他6名『奈良県高市郡志料』奈良県高市郡役所（編集・刊行）1915年、pp.299-300。 [[猪熊兼勝]]は、[[百済]][[益山市|益山]]の[[弥勒寺]]跡（[[:en:Mireuksa|英語版]]）の西石塔の四隅に置かれた石人像と類似し百済工人の技術が使用され、7世紀後半の作造と推定する猪熊兼勝「飛鳥猿石考」『奈良国立文化財研究所創立30周年記念論文集文化財論叢』1983年。 [[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。 == 高取の猿石 == 欽明陵付近の猿石を「平田の猿石」と呼ぶのに対し、高取山（奈良県高市郡[[高取町]]）中腹に「高取の猿石」と呼ばれる像高74cmの石像がある{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=小川光暘 |title=猿石の周辺(二) : 明日香研究ノートから |journal=人文學 |issue=122 |publisher= 同志社大学人文学会|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}。地元の伝承では池田から掘り出された猿石群の一つだったものが[[高取城]]の築城時に運び去られたものといわれているが、これを否定する学説もあり議論がある。池田から掘り出された猿石群の一つだったかは不明だが様式的にみて関連性があるとする説もある。 == 交通 == * [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[飛鳥駅]]徒歩10分 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥の石造物]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石] {{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:石像]]

revision.text:
{{Four images|right|Saruishi-1.jpg|120|Saruishi-2.jpg|120|{{center|上段：女（左）・山王権現（右）
下段：僧（左）・男（右）}}||Saruishi-3.jpg|Saruishi-4.jpg}} '''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]の[[吉備姫王]]墓内にある奇石。全4体。 == 概要 == [[梅山古墳]]（欽明天皇陵古墳）の南西には吉備姫墓とされる円墳（檜隈墓）があり、その墓域内に4体の石像が西向きに南北に並んでいる{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=飛鳥地域の古墳（西部・東部）|accessdate=2024年3月12日|publisher=奈良県教育委員会事務局 文化財保存課}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=遠藤祐二 |author2=加藤碵一 |title=飛鳥の石造物 |journal=地質ニュース |issue=592 |publisher= |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}。単に「[[花崗岩]]」とする文献もあるが、[[宮内庁]]の調査報告では材質は[[石英閃緑岩]]としている。 4体の像は北から順に通称で「女（[[女性]]）」・「[[山王権現]]」・「僧（[[法師]]）」・「男（[[男性]]）」と名付けられている。このうち「法師」については[[力士]]とする説もある。また「法師」像以外は裏面にも顔が彫られているとされるが、墓域のため柵があり現地では確認できない。 * 女（女性） - 高さ最大110cm、幅最大50cm、奥行最大110cm * 山王権現 - 高さ最大131cm、幅最大96cm、奥行最大82cm * 僧（法師） - 高さ最大114cm、幅最大74cm、奥行最大60cm * 男（男性） - 高さ最大88cm、幅最大64cm、奥行最大38cm [[猪熊兼勝]]は、[[百済]][[益山市|益山]]の[[弥勒寺]]跡（[[:en:Mireuksa|英語版]]）の西石塔の四隅に置かれた石人像と類似し百済工人の技術が使用され、7世紀後半の作造と推定する猪熊兼勝「飛鳥猿石考」『奈良国立文化財研究所創立30周年記念論文集文化財論叢』1983年。 文献上の初見は『[[今昔物語集]]』巻31にある「石ノ鬼形」とされ、その記述から欽明天皇陵の周濠のほとりにあったとみられる。 [[1702年]]（元禄15年）10月5日に梅山古墳の付近にあった坂合村大字平田字池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、古墳修築時にいったん他に移動させて、その後に現在の場所に移された今西伊之吉他6名『奈良県高市郡志料』奈良県高市郡役所（編集・刊行）1915年、pp.299-300。[[1791年]]（寛政3年）の『大和名所図会』によると、1702年（元禄15年）10月5日に欽明天皇陵の堤の南側から掘り出されて陵中に移されたとする。江戸後期になると、[[谷森善臣]]『藺笠のしづく』の安政4年（[[1857年]]）4月22日条で石像の位置は欽明天皇陵の南の下段となっている。さらに、[[1862年]]（文久2年）の修陵時の成功図では猿石は吉備姫王墓よりも南に柵を設けて囲われている。その後、[[1875年]]（明治8年）10月27日の[[教部省]]から奈良県への申し入れでは「吉備皇女御墓柵内」となっており、この頃までには吉備姫王墓内に移されたとみられる。 [[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。 == 所在地 == * 奈良県高市郡明日香村大字平田1659番の1{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=元明天皇陵内陵碑・那富山墓内「隼人石」・桧隈墓内「猿石」の保存処理及び調査報告|accessdate=2024年3月12日|publisher=宮内庁書陵部}} == 交通 == * [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[飛鳥駅]]徒歩10分 == 高取の猿石 == 欽明陵付近の猿石を「平田の猿石」と呼ぶのに対し、高取山（奈良県高市郡[[高取町]]）中腹に「高取の猿石」と呼ばれる像高74cmの石像がある{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=小川光暘 |title=猿石の周辺(二) : 明日香研究ノートから |journal=人文學 |issue=122 |publisher= 同志社大学人文学会|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}。地元の伝承では池田から掘り出された猿石群の一つだったものが[[高取城]]の築城時に運び去られたものといわれているが、これを否定する学説もあり議論がある。池田から掘り出された猿石群の一つだったかは不明だが様式的にみて関連性があるとする説もある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥の石造物]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石] {{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:石像]]

The group consists of four stone statues. == Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb p..."
revision.text:
'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]
[[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[ja:猿石]]

Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>

== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}

[[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[category:new pages]][[ja:猿石]]

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

revision.comment: 吉備姫王と円墳について
revision.text:
{{Four images|right|Saruishi-1.jpg|120|Saruishi-2.jpg|120|{{center|上段：女（左）・山王権現（右）
下段：僧（左）・男（右）}}||Saruishi-3.jpg|Saruishi-4.jpg}} '''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]の[[吉備姫王]]墓内にある奇石。全4体。 == 概要 == [[梅山古墳]]（欽明天皇陵古墳）の南西には[[吉備姫王]]墓とされる[[円墳]]（檜隈墓）があり、その墓域内に4体の石像が西向きに南北に並んでいる{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=飛鳥地域の古墳（西部・東部）|accessdate=2024年3月12日|publisher=奈良県教育委員会事務局 文化財保存課}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=遠藤祐二 |author2=加藤碵一 |title=飛鳥の石造物 |journal=地質ニュース |issue=592 |publisher= |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}。単に「[[花崗岩]]」とする文献もあるが、[[宮内庁]]の調査報告では材質は[[石英閃緑岩]]としている。 4体の像は北から順に通称で「女（[[女性]]）」・「[[山王権現]]」・「僧（[[法師]]）」・「男（[[男性]]）」と名付けられている。このうち「法師」については[[力士]]とする説もある。また「法師」像以外は裏面にも顔が彫られているとされるが、墓域のため柵があり現地では確認できない。 * 女（女性） - 高さ最大110cm、幅最大50cm、奥行最大110cm * 山王権現 - 高さ最大131cm、幅最大96cm、奥行最大82cm * 僧（法師） - 高さ最大114cm、幅最大74cm、奥行最大60cm * 男（男性） - 高さ最大88cm、幅最大64cm、奥行最大38cm [[猪熊兼勝]]は、[[百済]][[益山市|益山]]の[[弥勒寺]]跡（[[:en:Mireuksa|英語版]]）の西石塔の四隅に置かれた石人像と類似し百済工人の技術が使用され、7世紀後半の作造と推定する猪熊兼勝「飛鳥猿石考」『奈良国立文化財研究所創立30周年記念論文集文化財論叢』1983年。 [[1702年]]（元禄15年）10月5日に梅山古墳の付近にあった坂合村大字平田字池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、古墳修築時にいったん他に移動させて、その後に現在の場所に移された今西伊之吉他6名『奈良県高市郡志料』奈良県高市郡役所（編集・刊行）1915年、pp.299-300。 文献上の初見は『[[今昔物語集]]』巻31にある「石ノ鬼形」とされ、その記述から欽明天皇陵の周濠のほとりにあったとみられる。[[1791年]]（寛政3年）の『大和名所図会』によると、1702年（元禄15年）10月5日に欽明天皇陵の堤の南側から掘り出されて陵中に移されたとする。江戸後期になると、[[谷森善臣]]『藺笠のしづく』の安政4年（[[1857年]]）4月22日条で石像の位置は欽明天皇陵の南の下段となっている。さらに、[[1862年]]（文久2年）の修陵時の成功図では猿石は吉備姫王墓よりも南に柵を設けて囲われている。その後、[[1875年]]（明治8年）10月27日の[[教部省]]から奈良県への申し入れでは「吉備皇女御墓柵内」となっており、この頃までには吉備姫王墓内に移されたとみられる。 [[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。 == 所在地 == * 奈良県高市郡明日香村大字平田1659番の1{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=元明天皇陵内陵碑・那富山墓内「隼人石」・桧隈墓内「猿石」の保存処理及び調査報告|accessdate=2024年3月12日|publisher=宮内庁書陵部}} == 交通 == * [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[飛鳥駅]]徒歩10分 == 高取の猿石 == 欽明陵付近の猿石を「平田の猿石」と呼ぶのに対し、高取山（奈良県高市郡[[高取町]]）中腹に「高取の猿石」と呼ばれる像高74cmの石像がある{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=小川光暘 |title=猿石の周辺(二) : 明日香研究ノートから |journal=人文學 |issue=122 |publisher= 同志社大学人文学会|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}。地元の伝承では池田から掘り出された猿石群の一つだったものが[[高取城]]の築城時に運び去られたものといわれているが、これを否定する学説もあり議論がある。池田から掘り出された猿石群の一つだったかは不明だが様式的にみて関連性があるとする説もある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥の石造物]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石] {{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:石像]]

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.

Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.

== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

[[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Saruishi]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]]

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>

In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

[[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:Saruishi]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saruishi-qq]] [[Category:Saruishi]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:猿石]]

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

[[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:Saruishi]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saruishi-qq]] [[Category:Saruishi]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:猿石]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]]

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

revision.text:
'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]
[[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:Saruishi]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Saruishi-qq]] [[Category:Saruishi]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:猿石]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]]
