[[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:Saruishi]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq deprecated start]]<!--Saruishi-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Saruishi]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:猿石]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]]

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height qq0 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth qq0 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height qq4 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />

Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />

revision.text:
'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height qq0 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth qq0 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height qq4 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

revision.text:
'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height qq0 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth qq0 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height qq4 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

revision.text:
'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height qq0 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth qq0 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height qq4 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height qq0 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth qq0 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height qq4 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the burial mound, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}

The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s burial mound.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height qq0 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth qq0 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height qq4 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>

The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

[[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:Saruishi]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Saruishi}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] <!--Saruishi-->[[Category:Saruishi]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[ja:猿石]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:aruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Saruishi}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saruishi]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Saruishi}} [[Category:New]]

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791年}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}}== See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791st year }} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>

The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702nd year }} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791st year }} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875th year }} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

revision.text:
'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702nd year }} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791st year }} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875th year }} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]
<!--Saruishi-->
