== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=draft}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=draft}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市|12=draft}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702nd year|12=draft}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集|12=draft}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791st year|12=draft}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館|12=draft}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道|12=draft}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町|12=draft}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城|12=draft}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum] <!--Saruishi-->

revision.comment: Bot: single‑shot reformat + ill→|12=draft + draft categories + iws + DEFAULTSORT + move HTML comments + meta‑cat check
47 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:猿石 up to rev 103276413
revision.text:
{{Four images|right|Saruishi-1.jpg|120|Saruishi-2.jpg|120|{{center|上段：女（左）・山王権現（右）
下段：僧（左）・男（右）}}||Saruishi-3.jpg|Saruishi-4.jpg}} '''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]の[[吉備姫王]]墓内にある奇石。全4体。 == 概要 == [[梅山古墳]]（欽明天皇陵古墳）の南西には[[吉備姫王]]墓とされる[[円墳]]（檜隈墓）があり、その墓域内に4体の石像が西向きに南北に並んでいる{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=飛鳥地域の古墳（西部・東部）|accessdate=2024年3月12日|publisher=奈良県教育委員会事務局 文化財保存課}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=遠藤祐二 |author2=加藤碵一 |title=飛鳥の石造物 |journal=地質ニュース |issue=592 |publisher= |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}。単に「[[花崗岩]]」とする文献もあるが、[[宮内庁]]の調査報告では材質は[[石英閃緑岩]]としている。 4体の像は北から順に通称で「女（[[女性]]）」・「[[山王権現]]」・「僧（[[法師]]）」・「男（[[男性]]）」と名付けられている。このうち「法師」については[[力士]]とする説もある。また「法師」像以外は裏面にも顔が彫られているとされるが、墓域のため柵があり現地では確認できない。 * 女（女性） - 高さ最大110cm、幅最大50cm、奥行最大110cm * 山王権現 - 高さ最大131cm、幅最大96cm、奥行最大82cm * 僧（法師） - 高さ最大114cm、幅最大74cm、奥行最大60cm * 男（男性） - 高さ最大88cm、幅最大64cm、奥行最大38cm [[猪熊兼勝]]は、[[百済]][[益山市|益山]]の[[弥勒寺]]跡（[[:en:Mireuksa|英語版]]）の西石塔の四隅に置かれた石人像と類似し百済工人の技術が使用され、7世紀後半の作造と推定する猪熊兼勝「飛鳥猿石考」『奈良国立文化財研究所創立30周年記念論文集文化財論叢』1983年。 [[1702年]]（元禄15年）10月5日に梅山古墳の付近にあった坂合村大字平田字池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、古墳修築時にいったん他に移動させて、その後に現在の場所に移された今西伊之吉他6名『奈良県高市郡志料』奈良県高市郡役所（編集・刊行）1915年、pp.299-300。 文献上の初見は『[[今昔物語集]]』巻31にある「石ノ鬼形」とされ、その記述から欽明天皇陵の周濠のほとりにあったとみられる。[[1791年]]（寛政3年）の『大和名所図会』によると、1702年（元禄15年）10月5日に欽明天皇陵の堤の南側から掘り出されて陵中に移されたとする。江戸後期になると、[[谷森善臣]]『藺笠のしづく』の安政4年（[[1857年]]）4月22日条で石像の位置は欽明天皇陵の南の下段となっている。さらに、[[1862年]]（文久2年）の修陵時の成功図では猿石は吉備姫王墓よりも南に柵を設けて囲われている。その後、[[1875年]]（明治8年）10月27日の[[教部省]]から奈良県への申し入れでは「吉備皇女御墓柵内」となっており、この頃までには吉備姫王墓内に移されたとみられる。 [[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。 == 所在地 == * 奈良県高市郡明日香村大字平田1659番の1{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=元明天皇陵内陵碑・那富山墓内「隼人石」・桧隈墓内「猿石」の保存処理及び調査報告|accessdate=2024年3月12日|publisher=宮内庁書陵部}} == 交通 == * [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[飛鳥駅]]徒歩10分 == 高取の猿石 == 欽明陵付近の猿石を「平田の猿石」と呼ぶのに対し、高取山（奈良県高市郡[[高取町]]）中腹に「高取の猿石」と呼ばれる像高74cmの石像がある{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=小川光暘 |title=猿石の周辺(二) : 明日香研究ノートから |journal=人文學 |issue=122 |publisher= 同志社大学人文学会|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}。地元の伝承では池田から掘り出された猿石群の一つだったものが[[高取城]]の築城時に運び去られたものといわれているが、これを否定する学説もあり議論がある。池田から掘り出された猿石群の一つだったかは不明だが様式的にみて関連性があるとする説もある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥の石造物]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石] {{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:石像]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[猿石]] to [[Saruishi]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{Four images|right|Saruishi-1.jpg|120|Saruishi-2.jpg|120|{{center|上段：女（左）・山王権現（右）
下段：僧（左）・男（右）}}||Saruishi-3.jpg|Saruishi-4.jpg}} '''猿石'''（さるいし）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]の[[吉備姫王]]墓内にある奇石。全4体。 == 概要 == [[梅山古墳]]（欽明天皇陵古墳）の南西には[[吉備姫王]]墓とされる[[円墳]]（檜隈墓）があり、その墓域内に4体の石像が西向きに南北に並んでいる{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=飛鳥地域の古墳（西部・東部）|accessdate=2024年3月12日|publisher=奈良県教育委員会事務局 文化財保存課}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=遠藤祐二 |author2=加藤碵一 |title=飛鳥の石造物 |journal=地質ニュース |issue=592 |publisher= |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}。単に「[[花崗岩]]」とする文献もあるが、[[宮内庁]]の調査報告では材質は[[石英閃緑岩]]としている。 4体の像は北から順に通称で「女（[[女性]]）」・「[[山王権現]]」・「僧（[[法師]]）」・「男（[[男性]]）」と名付けられている。このうち「法師」については[[力士]]とする説もある。また「法師」像以外は裏面にも顔が彫られているとされるが、墓域のため柵があり現地では確認できない。 * 女（女性） - 高さ最大110cm、幅最大50cm、奥行最大110cm * 山王権現 - 高さ最大131cm、幅最大96cm、奥行最大82cm * 僧（法師） - 高さ最大114cm、幅最大74cm、奥行最大60cm * 男（男性） - 高さ最大88cm、幅最大64cm、奥行最大38cm [[猪熊兼勝]]は、[[百済]][[益山市|益山]]の[[弥勒寺]]跡（[[:en:Mireuksa|英語版]]）の西石塔の四隅に置かれた石人像と類似し百済工人の技術が使用され、7世紀後半の作造と推定する猪熊兼勝「飛鳥猿石考」『奈良国立文化財研究所創立30周年記念論文集文化財論叢』1983年。 [[1702年]]（元禄15年）10月5日に梅山古墳の付近にあった坂合村大字平田字池田という場所の田んぼから掘り出されて古墳の南側に置かれていたが、古墳修築時にいったん他に移動させて、その後に現在の場所に移された今西伊之吉他6名『奈良県高市郡志料』奈良県高市郡役所（編集・刊行）1915年、pp.299-300。 文献上の初見は『[[今昔物語集]]』巻31にある「石ノ鬼形」とされ、その記述から欽明天皇陵の周濠のほとりにあったとみられる。[[1791年]]（寛政3年）の『大和名所図会』によると、1702年（元禄15年）10月5日に欽明天皇陵の堤の南側から掘り出されて陵中に移されたとする。江戸後期になると、[[谷森善臣]]『藺笠のしづく』の安政4年（[[1857年]]）4月22日条で石像の位置は欽明天皇陵の南の下段となっている。さらに、[[1862年]]（文久2年）の修陵時の成功図では猿石は吉備姫王墓よりも南に柵を設けて囲われている。その後、[[1875年]]（明治8年）10月27日の[[教部省]]から奈良県への申し入れでは「吉備皇女御墓柵内」となっており、この頃までには吉備姫王墓内に移されたとみられる。 [[奈良文化財研究所]][[飛鳥資料館]]の庭には、レプリカが置かれており背面の様子なども観察できる。 == 所在地 == * 奈良県高市郡明日香村大字平田1659番の1{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=元明天皇陵内陵碑・那富山墓内「隼人石」・桧隈墓内「猿石」の保存処理及び調査報告|accessdate=2024年3月12日|publisher=宮内庁書陵部}} == 交通 == * [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[飛鳥駅]]徒歩10分 == 高取の猿石 == 欽明陵付近の猿石を「平田の猿石」と呼ぶのに対し、高取山（奈良県高市郡[[高取町]]）中腹に「高取の猿石」と呼ばれる像高74cmの石像がある{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=小川光暘 |title=猿石の周辺(二) : 明日香研究ノートから |journal=人文學 |issue=122 |publisher= 同志社大学人文学会|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}。地元の伝承では池田から掘り出された猿石群の一つだったものが[[高取城]]の築城時に運び去られたものといわれているが、これを否定する学説もあり議論がある。池田から掘り出された猿石群の一つだったかは不明だが様式的にみて関連性があるとする説もある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥の石造物]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html 飛鳥資料館 猿石] {{DEFAULTSORT:さるいし}} [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:石像]]

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集|qq=draft|qq=draft}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791st year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Saruishi}}
{{translated page|ja|猿石|version=103276413|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[sn:Saruishi]] [[ja:猿石]] [[sn:aruishi]] [[en:Saruishi]]
<!--Saruishi-->

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集|qq=draft|qq=draft}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791st year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Stone Monuments of Asuka|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]
{{translated page|ja|猿石|version=103276413|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:Saruishi]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]]
}}

== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Station|qq=}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu Railway|qq=}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatori, Nara|qq=}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatori Castle|qq=}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

[[sn:Saruishi]] [[ja:猿石]] [[sn:aruishi]] [[en:Saruishi]]
<!--Saruishi-->

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|qq=}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka, Nara|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefecture|qq=}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|qq=}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iksan|qq=}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1702|qq=}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|qq=}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1791|qq=}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1875|qq=}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Historical Museum|qq=}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Station|qq=}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu Railway|qq=}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatori, Nara|qq=}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatori Castle|qq=}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

In {{ill|1702|ja|1702nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1702|qq=}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. '''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|qq=}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka, Nara|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefecture|qq=}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|qq=}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|ja|益山市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iksan|qq=}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|ja|1702年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1702|qq=}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語集|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|qq=}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|ja|1791st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1791|qq=}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|ja|1875年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1875|qq=}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|ja|飛鳥資料館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Historical Museum|qq=}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|ja|飛鳥駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Station|qq=}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu Railway|qq=}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|ja|高取町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatori, Nara|qq=}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|ja|高取城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatori Castle|qq=}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]
{{translated page|ja|猿石|version=103276413|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 2]] }}

== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from [[:en:Asuka Station|Asuka Station]], [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]]
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in [[:en:Takatori, Nara|Takatori, Nara]], Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

revision.comment: Bot: remove invalid enwiki interwiki links
revision.text:
'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|qq=}}, situated in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|qq=}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in [[:en:Iksan|Iksan]], Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In [[:en:1702|1702]] (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatari]],'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In [[:en:1791|1791]] (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by [[:en:1875|1875]] (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the [[:en:Asuka Historical Museum|Asuka Historical Museum]], allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from [[:en:Asuka Station|Asuka Station]], [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]]
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in [[:en:Takatori, Nara|Takatori, Nara]], Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the [[:en:Asuka Historical Museum|Asuka Historical Museum]], allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from [[:en:Asuka Station|Asuka Station]], [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]]
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in [[:en:Takatori, Nara|Takatori, Nara]], Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|qq=}}, situated in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|qq=}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in [[:en:Iksan|Iksan]], Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In [[:en:1702|1702]] (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatari]],'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In [[:en:1791|1791]] (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by [[:en:1875|1875]] (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the [[:en:Asuka Historical Museum|Asuka Historical Museum]], allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from [[:en:Asuka Station|Asuka Station]], [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]]
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in [[:en:Takatori, Nara|Takatori, Nara]], Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}

{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|qq=|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in [[:en:Iksan|Iksan]], Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In [[:en:1702|1702]] (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatari]],'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In [[:en:1791|1791]] (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by [[:en:1875|1875]] (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the [[:en:Asuka Historical Museum|Asuka Historical Museum]], allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from [[:en:Asuka Station|Asuka Station]], [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]]
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in [[:en:Takatori, Nara|Takatori, Nara]], Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

The group consists of four stone statues.

{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|qq=|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in [[:en:Iksan|Iksan]], Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>

<!--Saruishi--> [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Saruishi}} {{wikidata link|Q11572150}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11572150]] Wikidata name: Saru-ishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|qq=|1=Princess Kibitsu-Hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412643}}, situated in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|qq=|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in [[:en:Iksan|Iksan]], Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In [[:en:1702|1702]] (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatari]],'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In [[:en:1791|1791]] (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by [[:en:1875|1875]] (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the [[:en:Asuka Historical Museum|Asuka Historical Museum]], allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from [[:en:Asuka Station|Asuka Station]], [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]]
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in [[:en:Takatori, Nara|Takatori, Nara]], Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata no short description - based on Wikidata English short description
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11572150]] Wikidata name: Saru-ishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|1=Princess Kibitsu-Hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412643}}, situated in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in [[:en:Iksan|Iksan]], Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In [[:en:1702|1702]] (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatari]],'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In [[:en:1791|1791]] (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by [[:en:1875|1875]] (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the [[:en:Asuka Historical Museum|Asuka Historical Museum]], allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from [[:en:Asuka Station|Asuka Station]], [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]]
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in [[:en:Takatori, Nara|Takatori, Nara]], Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatari]],'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In [[:en:1791|1791]] (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by [[:en:1875|1875]] (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the [[:en:Asuka Historical Museum|Asuka Historical Museum]], allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from [[:en:Asuka Station|Asuka Station]], [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]]
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in [[:en:Takatori, Nara|Takatori, Nara]], Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11572150]] Wikidata name: Saru-ishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|1=Princess Kibitsu-Hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412643}}, situated in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in [[:en:Iksan|Iksan]], Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In [[:en:1702|1702]] (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatari]],'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In [[:en:1791|1791]] (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by [[:en:1875|1875]] (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the [[:en:Asuka Historical Museum|Asuka Historical Museum]], allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>

The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
