== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in [[:en:Iksan|Iksan]], Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In [[:en:1702|1702]] (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatari]],'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In [[:en:1791|1791]] (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by [[:en:1875|1875]] (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the [[:en:Asuka Historical Museum|Asuka Historical Museum]], allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from [[:en:Asuka Station|Asuka Station]], [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]]
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in [[:en:Takatori, Nara|Takatori, Nara]], Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]
{{translated page|ja|猿石|version=103276413|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. {{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in [[:en:Iksan|Iksan]], Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>

Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatari]],'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In [[:en:1791|1791]] (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by [[:en:1875|1875]] (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />

== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in [[:en:Takatori, Nara|Takatori, Nara]], Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Saruishi}} {{wikidata link|Q11572150}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11572150]] Wikidata name: Saru-ishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|1=Princess Kibitsu-Hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412643}}, situated in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in [[:en:Iksan|Iksan]], Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In [[:en:1702|1702]] (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatari]],'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In [[:en:1791|1791]] (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by [[:en:1875|1875]] (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the [[:en:Asuka Historical Museum|Asuka Historical Museum]], allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from [[:en:Asuka Station|Asuka Station]], [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]]
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in [[:en:Takatori, Nara|Takatori, Nara]], Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of [[:en:Takatori Castle|Takatori Castle]], although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in [[:en:Iksan|Iksan]], Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>

The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|en|Iksan|WD=Q42137|lt=Iksan}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|en|1702|WD=Q6819|lt=1702}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatari}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|en|1791|WD=Q7784|lt=1791}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|en|1875|WD=Q7764|lt=1875}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|en|Asuka Historical Museum|WD=Q4811853|lt=Asuka Historical Museum}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>

== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|en|Takatori, Nara|WD=Q1202681|lt=Takatori, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|en|Takatori Castle|WD=Q5369622|lt=Takatori Castle}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|en|Iksan|WD=Q42137|lt=Iksan}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|en|1702|WD=Q6819|lt=1702}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatari}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|en|1791|WD=Q7784|lt=1791}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|en|1875|WD=Q7764|lt=1875}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|en|Asuka Historical Museum|WD=Q4811853|lt=Asuka Historical Museum}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|en|Asuka Station|WD=Q4811860|lt=Asuka Station}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|en|Takatori, Nara|WD=Q1202681|lt=Takatori, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|en|Takatori Castle|WD=Q5369622|lt=Takatori Castle}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]
{{translated page|ja|猿石|version=103276413|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. {{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|en|Iksan|WD=Q42137|lt=Iksan}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|en|1702|WD=Q6819|lt=1702}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. == Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|1=Princess Kibitsu-Hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412643}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />

{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|en|Iksan|WD=Q42137|lt=Iksan}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|en|1702|WD=Q6819|lt=1702}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>

Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|en|Asuka Historical Museum|WD=Q4811853|lt=Asuka Historical Museum}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>

In {{ill|1702|en|1702|WD=Q6819|lt=1702}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatari}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|en|1791|WD=Q7784|lt=1791}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|en|1875|WD=Q7764|lt=1875}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|en|Asuka Historical Museum|WD=Q4811853|lt=Asuka Historical Museum}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|en|Asuka Station|WD=Q4811860|lt=Asuka Station}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|en|Takatori, Nara|WD=Q1202681|lt=Takatori, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|en|Takatori Castle|WD=Q5369622|lt=Takatori Castle}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]]
'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|1=Princess Kibihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412643}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara|1=Asuka, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|en|Iksan|WD=Q42137|lt=Iksan|1=Iksan}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|en|1702|WD=Q6819|lt=1702|1=1702}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatari|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|en|1791|WD=Q7784|lt=1791|1=1791}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|en|1875|WD=Q7764|lt=1875|1=1875}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|en|Asuka Historical Museum|WD=Q4811853|lt=Asuka Historical Museum|1=Asuka Historical Museum}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|en|Asuka Station|WD=Q4811860|lt=Asuka Station|1=Asuka Station}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|en|Takatori, Nara|WD=Q1202681|lt=Takatori, Nara|1=Takatori, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|en|Takatori Castle|WD=Q5369622|lt=Takatori Castle|1=Takatori Castle}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Saruishi}} {{wikidata link|Q11572150}} [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Saruishi]][[ja:猿石]]
'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|1=Princess Kibihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412643}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara|1=Asuka, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|en|Iksan|WD=Q42137|lt=Iksan|1=Iksan}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|en|1702|WD=Q6819|lt=1702|1=1702}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatari|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|en|1791|WD=Q7784|lt=1791|1=1791}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|en|1875|WD=Q7764|lt=1875|1=1875}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|en|Asuka Historical Museum|WD=Q4811853|lt=Asuka Historical Museum|1=Asuka Historical Museum}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|en|Asuka Station|WD=Q4811860|lt=Asuka Station|1=Asuka Station}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|en|Takatori, Nara|WD=Q1202681|lt=Takatori, Nara|1=Takatori, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|en|Takatori Castle|WD=Q5369622|lt=Takatori Castle|1=Takatori Castle}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Monuments of Asuka|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Saruishi}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/saruisi.html Saruishi at the Asuka Historical Museum]

'''Saruishi''' (猿石) are curious stone figures located within the tomb precinct of {{ill|Kibihime no Ō|ja|吉備姫王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibihime no Ō|1=Princess Kibihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412643}}, situated in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara|1=Asuka, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The group consists of four stone statues.
== Overview == Near the {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun), there is a circular tomb believed to be the burial site of Kibihime no Ō, known as the Hinokuma Tomb. Within the tomb precinct, four stone statues are arranged facing west, aligned from north to south.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/57354/3.pdf|title=Kofun in the Asuka Region (West and East)|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Nara Prefectural Board of Education, Cultural Properties Division}}</ref><ref name="endo">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://www.gsj.jp/data/chishitsunews/03_12_07.pdf|author1=Yūji Endō |author2=Seiichi Katō |title=Stone Monuments of Asuka |journal=Chishitsu News|issue=592 |date=2003-12 |pages=53-60}}</ref> Although some references describe the material simply as granite,<ref name="endo" /> the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} survey identifies the stone as quartz diorite.<ref name="chosa" />
The four statues are colloquially named “Woman (female),” “{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}},” “Monk (priest),” and “Man (male),” ordered from north to south.<ref name="endo" /><ref name="chosa" /> The "priest" statue is alternatively interpreted as a wrestler in some theories.<ref name="endo" /> Except for the "priest" statue, the others are said to have faces carved on their reverse sides, although they are not visible due to the fence enclosing the tomb precinct.<ref name="endo" /> * Woman (female) - Maximum height 110 cm, maximum width 50 cm, maximum depth 110 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Sannō Gongen - Maximum height 131 cm, maximum width 96 cm, maximum depth 82 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Monk (priest) - Maximum height 114 cm, maximum width 74 cm, maximum depth 60 cm<ref name="chosa" /> * Man (male) - Maximum height 88 cm, maximum width 64 cm, maximum depth 38 cm<ref name="chosa" />
{{ill|Kanekatsu Inokuma|ja|猪熊兼勝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanekatsu Inokuma|1=Kanekatsu Inokuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66365172}} theorizes that the statues resemble the stone figures placed at the four corners of the western stone pagoda at the Mireuksa temple site in {{ill|Iksan|en|Iksan|WD=Q42137|lt=Iksan|1=Iksan}}, Baekje ({{ill|Mireuksa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mireuksa|qq=}}). He suggests that the statues were crafted using Baekje techniques and date to the latter half of the 7th century.<ref>Kanekatsu Inokuma, “A Study of the Saruishi in Asuka,” ''Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties 30th Anniversary Commemorative Essays on Cultural Properties,'' 1983.</ref>
In {{ill|1702|en|1702|WD=Q6819|lt=1702|1=1702}} (Genroku 15), the statues were excavated from rice paddies in the Sakai Village Hirata area near the Umemiyama Kofun. Initially placed south of the Kofun, they were moved during mound repairs before being relocated to their current positions.<ref>Imanishi Izo et al., ''Nara Prefecture Takagi County Historical Resources,'' Nara Prefecture Takagi County Office (Editor and Publisher), 1915, pp. 299-300.</ref>
The first textual mention appears in the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatari|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū}},'' Volume 31, as "stone demon shapes," suggesting they were located along the outer moat of Emperor Kinmei’s Kofun.<ref name="chosa" /> In {{ill|1791|en|1791|WD=Q7784|lt=1791|1=1791}} (Kansei 3), the ''Yamato Meisho Zue'' recorded their excavation on October 5, 1702, and relocation to the tomb precinct.<ref name="chosa" /> By the late Edo period, the stone statues were described in works such as the ''Drips of the Reed Hat'' (Ansei 4, 1857), and later accounts place them within the Kibihime no Ō tomb precinct by {{ill|1875|en|1875|WD=Q7764|lt=1875|1=1875}} (Meiji 8).<ref name="chosa" />
Reproductions of the statues are displayed in the garden of the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|en|Asuka Historical Museum|WD=Q4811853|lt=Asuka Historical Museum|1=Asuka Historical Museum}}, allowing visitors to observe the reverse sides of the figures.
== Location == * 1659-1 Hirata, Asuka Village, Takagi District, Nara Prefecture<ref name="chosa">{{Cite web|url=https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp/Publication/PDF/000/kiyo051b001.pdf|title=Preservation and Survey Report on Monuments in Emperor Genshō’s Tomb and Kibihime Tomb|accessdate=2024-03-12|publisher=Imperial Household Agency, Shoryobu}}</ref>
== Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Asuka Station|en|Asuka Station|WD=Q4811860|lt=Asuka Station|1=Asuka Station}}, {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}}
== Saruishi of Takatori == While the Saruishi near Emperor Kinmei’s tomb are known as the “Hirata Saruishi,” a similar stone figure, called the “Saruishi of Takatori,” is located on the slopes of Mount Takatori in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|en|Takatori, Nara|WD=Q1202681|lt=Takatori, Nara|1=Takatori, Nara}}, Nara Prefecture. The 74 cm-tall statue is said to have been relocated during the construction of {{ill|Takatori Castle|en|Takatori Castle|WD=Q5369622|lt=Takatori Castle|1=Takatori Castle}}, although this claim is debated.<ref name="ogawa">{{Cite journal |language=ja|url=https://doi.org/10.14988/pa.2017.0000002835|author=Mitsuyō Ogawa |title=Notes on Saruishi |journal=Humanities|issue=122 |publisher=Doshisha University|date=1972-03-25 |pages=1-14}}</ref> Stylistic similarities suggest a possible connection to the Hirata Saruishi.<ref name="ogawa" />
